coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of zwitterionic carbon quantum dots in cellulose acetate tubular membrane for oil/water separation

Yang, Hong-Li
Hsu, Yu-Xin
Leron, Rhoda B.
Tsai, Hui-An
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
coredata.dc:description: The substantial generation of oily industrial wastewater poses a considerable environmental threat. A promising strategy for addressing this challenge involves employing ultrafiltration membranes integrated with a hydrophilic nanomaterial. This study aims to investigate the properties of zwitterionic carbon quantum dots (ZQDs) and enhance the performance of cellulose acetate (CA) tubular membranes by incorporating ZQDs. The CA tubular membrane was crafted using the dry-wet spinning method. The introduction of ZQDs into the CA casting solution disrupted its thermodynamic stability and expedited the non-solvent inflow rate during membrane formation, thereby augmenting both membrane porosity and pore size. The inclusion of ZQDs heightened the membrane's hydrophilicity owing to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups in the nanomaterial. The modified tubular membrane demonstrated high pure water and diesel oil-water emulsion permeate flux of 581.3 ± 33.9 L m−1 h−1 bar−1 and 350.5 ± 11.2 L m−1 h−1 bar−1, respectively, with an impressive oil rejection rate of 98 %. The flux recovery and reversible fouling ratio increased to 88.42 % and 28.12 %, respectively, while the irreversible fouling ratio decreased to 11.57 %. The modified tubular membrane effectively rejected various oil-water emulsions (diesel oil, dodecane, canola oil, and engine oil) with a separation efficiency ranging from 94 % to 99 %. In conclusion, the zwitterionic-modified tubular membrane proved to be effective in the separation of oil and water.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The substantial generation of oily industrial wastewater poses a considerable environmental threat. A promising strategy for addressing this challenge involves employing ultrafiltration membranes integrated with a hydrophilic nanomaterial. This study aims to investigate the properties of zwitterionic carbon quantum dots (ZQDs) and enhance the performance of cellulose acetate (CA) tubular membranes by incorporating ZQDs. The CA tubular membrane was crafted using the dry-wet spinning method. The introduction of ZQDs into the CA casting solution disrupted its thermodynamic stability and expedited the non-solvent inflow rate during membrane formation, thereby augmenting both membrane porosity and pore size. The inclusion of ZQDs heightened the membrane's hydrophilicity owing to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups in the nanomaterial. The modified tubular membrane demonstrated high pure water and diesel oil-water emulsion permeate flux of 581.3 ± 33.9 L m−1 h−1 bar−1 and 350.5 ± 11.2 L m−1 h−1 bar−1, respectively, with an impressive oil rejection rate of 98 %. The flux recovery and reversible fouling ratio increased to 88.42 % and 28.12 %, respectively, while the irreversible fouling ratio decreased to 11.57 %. The modified tubular membrane effectively rejected various oil-water emulsions (diesel oil, dodecane, canola oil, and engine oil) with a separation efficiency ranging from 94 % to 99 %. In conclusion, the zwitterionic-modified tubular membrane proved to be effective in the separation of oil and water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oil is an extreme heat reserve extensively utilized in transportation, mechanical and chemical manufacturing, and oil and gas industries [1–3]. These industries produce oily wastewater, which leads to environmental pollution and huge economic losses, damages aquatic resources, and threatens human health [4,5]. Conventional methods for treating oily wastewater include gravity separation, flotation, coagulation, and flocculation; however, they require high costs and demonstrate low separation efficiency [6–8]. Oil-water separation is affected by the size of the oil droplet and its stability in water. Oil droplets are categorized into three types: free oil (>150 μm), dispersed oil (20–150 μm), and emulsified oil (<20 μm) [9]. Among these, emulsified oil is the most difficult to treat using common methods due to its small size and high stability in water [10]. For such systems, membrane separation techniques like ultrafiltration (UF) are considered more effective, economical, and environment-friendly alternatives [11].

Polymeric membranes are widely used in ultrafiltration for treating oily wastewater due to their cost-effectiveness and ease of production [12]. Cellulose acetate (CA) is a biodegradable polymeric membrane that offers impressive chemical and mechanical stability, efficient transport properties, low toxicity, and good water affinity. Its low cost and sustainability could promote ease of commercialization, making it ideal for oily wastewater treatment applications [13,14]. However, one disadvantage of CA membranes is its susceptibility to fouling [15], which can reduce separation efficiency, shorten membrane lifespan, and increase operational costs due to the need for extensive membrane cleaning or replacement.

Recent researches reported that incorporating hydrophilic carbon-based nanomaterials (graphene oxide (GO) [16–18], carbon nanotubes (CNT) [19,20], and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [21,22]) is a promising approach for attenuation of membrane fouling [23–25]. Unlike GO sheets, 2-dimensional carbon-based materials, CQDs possess valuable properties for integration into advanced membranes, primarily due to their small size (zero-dimensional) and numerous surface functional groups that can form chemical bonds with other substances [26]. CQDs are produced easily and cost-effectively, and their physicochemical characteristics, such as exceptional small size, biocompatibility, remarkable chemical stability, low-toxicity hydrophilicity, abundant surface functional groups, and resistance to fouling, are highly desirable [27–29]. Furthermore, their specific sizes and shapes enable effective dispersion in polar solvents and polymer matrices, which is crucial for their use in membrane production and separation processes [30]. CQDs feature a significant amount of epoxy, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups on their surfaces, ensuring outstanding water solubility and providing versatile capabilities for further functionalization and surface treatments [31]. Several research studies have highlighted the potential of utilizing CQDs as effective nanofillers in thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes, which exhibit excellent characteristics such as considerable water and permeation flux and strong resistance to fouling [32–34]. Zheng et al. [35] reported that the zwitterionization of CQDs enhanced the antifouling property of TFN membranes. They synthesized zwitterionic polyethyleneimine polymer via a ring-opening reaction of propanesultone followed by a quaternization reaction of polyethyleneimine (PEI). Then, zwitterionic polyethyleneimine carbon dots (ZPEI-CDs) nanoparticles were synthesized via the hydrothermal “bottom-up” approach of CA and ZPEI. The incorporation of ZPEI-CDs in the TFN membrane was done by interfacial polymerization. After the nanoparticles were incorporated, the zwitterionic (charged monomers) and hydrophilic functional groups allowed the repulsion of foulants in the membrane surface. The permeability and salt rejection were increased because of the electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance effect. The antifouling properties of the TFN-ZPEI-CDs membrane were also enhanced. Liu et al. [36] explained the general antifouling mechanisms of the zwitterionic group in membranes. It primarily involves creating a hydration layer on the membrane surface, which raises the energy barrier against fouling agents and steric hindrance. It increases the Gibbs free energy and hinders pollutant attachment to the membrane surface, enhancing the separation efficiency.

Reinforced hollow fiber membranes (HFMs), also known as tubular membranes, propose several advantages over traditional flat-sheet membranes. These include high packing density, self-supporting mechanical properties to withstand the backwashing process, and increased surface area of the membrane per unit volume of membrane modules, resulting in enhanced efficiency [37,38]. A standard method of reinforced HFMs in industrial applications is braid-reinforced HFMs. It involves a tubular braid substrate serving as the reinforcement material coated by a polymer solution or a porous layer applied onto the exterior of the tubular substrate [39]. Braid-reinforced HFMs are easily commercialized and applicable on an industrial scale, increasing the mechanical strength and durability of the membranes, resulting in a thinner filtration layer that reduces filtration resistance and increases flux and high separation performance [40].

While there have been studies concerning the zwitterionic modification of CQDs available in the literature [35,41,42], there is a lack of further investigation regarding their application in oil/water separation using tubular membranes. The main objective of this study was to enhance the separation efficiency and antifouling properties of CA tubular membranes (braid-reinforced HFMs) for oil/water separation. Zwitterionic carbon quantum dots (ZQDs) nanoparticles were synthesized by the reaction of CQDs with 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC) followed by a ring-opening reaction via 1,4-butanesultone. These novel ZQDs nanoparticles exhibited increased hydrophilicity and were incorporated into CA tubular membranes to attain improved hydrophilicity, excellent separation efficiency, and enhanced antifouling behavior. The ZQDs incorporated in CA tubular membrane was fabricated using the dry-wet spinning method, and their permeation and antifouling properties were subsequently evaluated.

CA powder (CA-394-60S) was procured from Eastman Chemical Company (Palo Alto, CA, USA). N- Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99 % purity) and dodecane (95 %) were acquired from Tedia Company, Inc. (Fairfield, OH, United States). Polyester (PET) substrate (diameter = 1.8 mm) was obtained from King Membrane Energy Technology Inc. (Tainan, Taiwan). Citric acid (anhydrous, 99.5 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, 99.5 % purity) were purchased from Showa Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, analytical grade) was obtained from Aencore Chemical Pty., Ltd. (Melbourne, Australia). Hydrochloride salt of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC.HCL, 98 %), and 1, 4-butanesultone (BS, 99 %) were procured from Alfa Aesar Co. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Ethanol (95 %) was procured from Echo Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tainan, Taiwan). Canola oil, engine oil, and diesel oil were obtained from Weiyi Enterprises Company Ltd. (Kaohsiung, Taiwan), Exxon Mobil Corporation (Spring, TX, United States), and CPC Corporation Taiwan (Chinese Petroleum Corporation, Taiwan), respectively.

The CQD nanoparticles were synthesized using the method presented in our previous study [43]. The carbon source used in the study was citric acid. It undergoes pyrolysis, leading to the creation of structures that are rich in carbon [44,45]. Citric acid adds functional groups, such as carboxyl groups, to the surface of carbon quantum dots which is crucial for subsequent functionalization. Two grams of citric acid were added to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave and heated in an oven at 210 °C for 3 h. The white solid citric acid was concentrated into an orange paste, which indicated the successful synthesis of CQDs. The CQDs were added into 20 mL of DI water and the pH was adjusted to 7 using 0.25 M NaOH. Afterward, the volume was adjusted to 100 mL using DI water. The CQDs solution was purified using a dialysis tube for 6 h, where the water was changed every 1 h. The solution was then placed in a rotary evaporator and freeze-dried for 24 h to collect the dried CQDs powder.

Two-hundred thirty mg of EDC was added in 2 mL 10 wt% PBS aqueous solution. Then, 1 g of CQDs was added into the solution and was placed in a sonicator for 1 min. Afterwards, the volume was adjusted to 40 mL by adding DI water and the pH was adjusted to 4 using 0.1 M HCl. The aqueous excess EDC solution was sealed in a vacuum bottle and stored in a dark environment at room temperature for 4 h. Subsequently, the EDC solution underwent dialysis for 30 min, to purify the solution and stop the reaction, where the water was changed every 10 min. Next, the solution was placed in a rotary evaporator and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain dried quantum dots powder. The tertiary amine (transitional) carbon quantum dots were represented as TQDs. A 0.08 g of 1,4-BS was added to 50 mL of 50 v/v% ethanol/water solution. Then, 0.5 g of TQDs was placed in the solution and stored for 11 h under room temperature. Same purification and dehydration processes were done as the TQDs. The dried zwitterionic carbon quantum dots powder was represented as ZQDs powder. The mechanism of zwitterionization of CQDs is shown in Figure S1
[46]. Through the tertiary amine (CN) area obtained by the XPS N1 narrow scan of TQD and the CN+ area of the N1 narrow scan of ZQD, the zwitterionic degree of the quaternization (CN+) of tertiary amine (CN) can be calculated using the following equation [47,48]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pristine CA and CA hybrid membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine CA and hybrid tubular membranes were fabricated by the dry-wet spinning method ( Figure S2 ). First, the quantum dots was dispersed in NMP using a homogenizer at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. Then, CA was added into the NMP solution at a fixed concentration of 15 wt%. After complete dissolution, the CA/NMP solution was degassed overnight. The tubular membranes were fabricated at a take-up speed and membrane thickness of 1.0 m/min and 225 µm, respectively. The pristine CA and hybrid tubular membranes were washed multiple times using distilled water to remove the excess NMP. Finally, the tubular membranes were stored in DI water for performance test and characterization. The compositions of the polymer casting solutions are summarized in Table 1 .

The functional groups of the quantum dots and prepared membranes were analyzed using attenuated total reflectance–Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Spectrum One FTIR Spectrometer, PerkinElmer, MA, USA). The elemental analysis was identified using X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, VG K-Alpha, ThermoFisher Scientific Inc., United Kingdom).

The morphology and size of the quantum dots were obtained using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100, Tokyo, Japan). Field emission scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive X-ray (FESEM-EDX Model S-4800, Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan) was used to analyze the cross-section and surface morphology of membranes. ImageJ software was utilized to measure the pore size and surface porosity of the membrane surface. The viscosities of the polymer dope solutions were determined using a rheometer (Brookfield R/S, AMETEK Brookfield, Middleboro, MA, USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM, NanoScope V, Bruker Scientific Instruments, MA, USA) was utilized to examine the surface roughness of membranes. The hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of each membrane were measured using a static contact angle (PD-VP Type, FACE Automatic Interfacial Tensiometer, Kyowa Interface Science Co. Ltd., Saitama, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase inversion mechanism of the dope solution
ce:section.ce:para: The viscosity of the dope solution was determined using a rheometer (Brookfield R/S-cps, MA, USA) equipped with a C50-1 spindle at a temperature of 25 °C. The thermodynamic behavior was observed in cloud-point curve from the ternary phase diagram. The cloud-point measurement of the dope solution was investigated using the titration method at a temperature of 25 °C. The kinetic behavior was investigated by correlating the viscosity of the dope solution with light transmittance experiment. The non-solvent used was water at a temperature of 25 °C, and the experimental setup was enclosed within a black box. The dope solution was cast onto the glass plate and submerged in the water coagulation bath. During the phase inversion process, the light transmittance was measured using Lutron software/RS232 (Model: Lutron LX-1102 light meter).

Figure S3 shows the tubular membrane module for crossflow filtration. The tubular membrane had an effective length of 15 cm. Ultrafiltration (UF) performance was measured using a laboratory crossflow setup ( Figure S4 ). The pure water flux, oil–water separation performance, and antifouling properties were determined using this setup. For the diesel oil–water emulsion, 1 g/L of SDS was added and stirred at 1500 rpm for 8 h. The weight ratio of diesel oil and SDS to water was 1:99. The permeation flux (J, L m−1 h−1 bar−1, LMHB) was calculated at 0.25 bar using the following equation:

where V (L) was the volume collected in permeate; A (m2) was the effective area of the membrane; and t (h) was the sampling time. Total organic carbon analyzer (Vario TOC Select, Elementar, Langenselbold, Germany) was used to determine the permeate and feed concentrations. The oil rejection rate (%R) was calculated using the following equation:

where Cp and Cf were the permeate and feed concentrations, respectively.

The crossflow system was allowed to reach steady state (P = 0.25 bar; flow rate = 1 L/min) for 30 min. The pure water flux (PWF) was measured for 1 h at 10-min intervals. Then, the feed was changed to oil emulsion, and the permeation flux was measured for 1 h at 10-min intervals. After 1 cycle, the membrane module was removed and the system was continuously washed with 1000 ppm SDS for 10 min, then with DI water for another 10 min. After which, the tubular membrane was simple washed with water for 10 min. The cycle of PWF, oil separation, and washing were repeated for 3.5 cycles. The flux recovery ratio (FRR, %), total fouling ratio (Rt, %), reversible fouling ratio (Rr, %), and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir, %) were calculated using the following equations:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and chemical analysis of quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of quantum dots are spherical or quasi-spherical structures and vary from 2 to 20 nm in size [26,27]. Figure S5 shows the morphology and size distribution of CQDs and modified quantum dots (TQDs and ZQDs). The mean diameters of CQDs, TQDs, and ZQDs were 2.88 ± 1.14, 2.96 ± 1.23, and 3.19 ± 1.36 nm, respectively. As shown in Figure S6 , the quantum dots emitted a bright blue color under UV irradiation light, demonstrating strong quantum confinement effect in the quantum dots [49].

The ATR-FTIR spectra of quantum dots (Fig. S7 (a)) exhibited the following functional groups: CO vibrations at 1076 cm−1; OH vibrations at 1105 cm−1; COC vibrations at 1257 cm−1; COH vibrations at 1387 cm−1; OCO vibrations at 1554 cm−1; CO stretch at 1705 cm−1; OH stretch at 3300 cm−1 [21]. After the EDC bonded with the CQDs, new characteristic peaks at 1179 cm−1, 1483 cm−1, and 2974 cm−1 were observed corresponding to CN stretching, scissoring of CH2 [50], and stretching vibrations of CH3 [51], respectively, which are all adjacent to tertiary amine from N-acylurea. The zwitterionization of quantum dots was achieved after the ring opening reaction and quaternization of N-acylurea. The following characteristic peaks were observed for the obtained ZQDs: CN stretching of quaternary amine at 911 cm−1 [52]; asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations of SO3 − at 1040 cm−1 and 1181 cm−1 [53,54]; CH stretching of quaternary amine at 2877 cm−1.

The XPS data for the quantum dots are shown in terms of survey spectra (Fig. S7 (b)) and elemental composition ( Table S1 ). After the reaction of EDC with CQDs, the oxygen concentration decreased and the nitrogen concentration increased. Furthermore, a new nitrogen peak was present in the TQDs survey scan, which confirmed the successful synthesis of TQDs. The zwitterionization of quantum dots was proven by the presence of sulfur in the ZQDs survey spectrum. The C1s narrow scan of CQDs is shown in Fig. S7(c), where the CC, CO, and CO peaks were located at 284, 285.7, and 287.4 eV, respectively. The N1 narrow scan of TQDs (Fig. S7(d)) had two peaks at 398.9 and 401.2 eV relating to CN [55] and NHCO bonds [56], respectively. This result further confirmed the reaction of EDC with CQDs. The binding energies at 398.8, 400.9, and 401.2 eV of ZQDs N1 narrow scan (Fig. S7(e)) correspond to CN, NHCO, and CN+ bonds [51], respectively. The new characteristic peak of CN+ indicated the successful zwitterionization of quantum dots. The quaternization (CN+) of tertiary amine (CN) was calculated using equation (1) [47]. The new peak of CN+ had an area fit percentage of 11.0 % whereas the CN peak decreased from 35.1 % to 29.6 %. The calculated zwitterionic degree of ZQDs was 27.0 %.

The morphologies of the pristine and hybrid CA tubular membranes are displayed in Fig. 1 . Based on the cross-sectional SEM images (Fig. 1 (a1-d1)), an asymmetric finger-like morphology was observed in the pristine and hybrid tubular membrane indicating instantaneous demixing where faster solvent and non-solvent exchange rate occurs during membrane formation. Fig. 1 (a3-d3) shows that porous membranes were fabricated when hydrophilic quantum dots were incorporated into the membrane. As we integrated hydrophilic quantum dots into the polymer solution, the non-solvent inflow rate accelerated during the membrane formation, developing a porous membrane surface [23].

The pore size, surface porosity, and skin-layer thickness of membranes are tabulated in Table 2 . The pore size and surface porosity of the membranes was increased as incorporating the quantum dots into the dope. The pore size and surface porosity trend was CA < CQDs/CA < TQDs/CA < ZQDs/CA. The membranes with quantum dots were more porous than pristine CA because they contained hydrophilic functional groups. CQDs/CA contained carboxylic groups, while TQDs/CA had both carboxylic and amine groups. Therefore, TQDs/CA was more porous because it included multiple hydrophilic groups, affecting the non-solvent inflow rate during membrane formation. ZQDs/CA was the most porous among all the membranes because it contained carboxylic, amine, anionic, and cationic groups. Furthermore, the electrostatic-induced hydration caused by the zwitterionic group enhanced the water affinity of the ZQDs/CA, which resulted in a porous surface of the membrane [57]. Furthermore, the skin-layer thickness followed the opposite trend with the pore size and surface porosity. This result can be attributed to the instantaneous demixing of non-solvent and solvent during membrane formation due to the hydrophilic nanomaterial in the casting solution [43].

The AFM images are shown in Fig. 1 (a4-d4) and the surface roughness parameters are listed in Table 2 . In the AFM images, the brightest area shows the highest surface peak while the darkest area depicts the pore on the membrane surface [58]. The surface roughness followed the same trend with the pore size and surface porosity. Generally, surface roughness is correlated with surface porosity. Membranes with high surface roughness correspond to high surface porosity [24]. The pristine CA had a low surface roughness of 4.52 ± 0.06 nm while the ZQDs/CA had the highest surface roughness of 6.08 ± 0.15 nm.

The light transmission experiment (Fig. 2
(a)) provides information about the demixing rate during the membrane formation. When the light transmittance of the cast film decreases rapidly, it means that the demixing rate increases. All the casting films exhibited the typical instantaneous demixing, leading to the formation of macrovoids (Fig. 1). Therefore, the presence of quantum dots does not significantly affect the formation of macrovoids in the CA membranes. Fig. 2 also shows that the demixing rate of the CA casting film with quantum dots added is slightly slower than that of the CA casting film without quantum dots, and the demixing rate of the CA film with ZQDs is the slowest. These results might be due to the influence of the casting solution viscosity (Table 1). This is consistent with previous studies, where the light transmission experiment revealed that the demixing rate increases with a decrease in the light transmittance of the casting film. The viscosity of the casting solution is a key factor in this process, with higher viscosity causing a slower exchange rate between solvent and non-solvent, resulting in a slower demixing rate [43]. Pore size and surface porosity were factors affected by membrane thermodynamic instability, which was studied in the following section.

The thermodynamic behavior of membrane formation is shown in Fig. 2 (b). When the quantum dots were added into the CA dope solution, there was an increase in the demixing gap. This result is because of the disruption of thermodynamic stability and reduction of miscibility of the polymer dope solution. Instantaneous liquid–liquid demixing leads to the formation of a membrane characterized by a thin top layer with a macrovoid structure; this phenomenon can be explained by enhancing the thermodynamic parameter [59]. Instantaneous demixing intensifies thermodynamic instability, leading to a higher thermodynamic parameter value corresponding to a membrane with larger pore size and surface porosity. The thermodynamic behavior explains the high surface porosity, large pore size, and thin skin-layer thickness of hybrid membranes because of the disruption of its thermodynamic stability during membrane formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical analysis and surface properties of membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The results of ATR-FTIR analysis for pristine CA and quantum dots/CA tubular membranes are presented in Fig. 3
(a). The stretching of OH group of CA was identified at 3469 cm−1. The distinct peaks related to the stretching of CH and vibrations of CO and CH groups were observed at 2952 cm−1, 1742 cm−1, and 1369 cm−1, respectively [60]. Furthermore, the vibration peaks at 1229 cm−1 and 1042 cm−1 indicated the symmetric connection of cellulosic esters (-ROR) [61]. Other than that, the ATR-FTIR spectra of hybrid membranes closely resembled those of the pristine CA and exhibited negligible changes. The ATR-FTIR could only identify functional groups within 1 µm from the membrane active layer [62]. The functional groups of quantum dots nanoparticles, including carboxyl, amine, and sulfonate, were undetectable due to the low concentration of quantum dots incorporation. Moreover, their peaks might overlap with those linked to the functional groups of CA. Consequently, EDX and XPS analyses were also employed to validate the presence of quantum dots incorporated in the CA membrane.

Table S2 and Fig. 3 (b) display the elemental composition and XPS survey of CA tubular membranes. The pristine membrane did not exhibit any detectable N or S element. Upon the addition of CQDs to the CA membrane, the resulting CQDs/CA tubular membrane demonstrated an altered ratio of C to O. This change can be attributed to an increase in oxygen from the carboxyl groups originating from the CQDs. When TQDs were incorporated, the TQDs/CA tubular membrane showcased an N content of 0.78 %. This increase was ascribed to amino groups from the tertiary amine of N-acylurea in the TQDs. Subsequently, with the addition of ZQDs, the resultant membranes, ZQDs/CA, exhibited both N (0.80 %) and S (0.17 %) elements. The N was from the amino groups from the quaternary amine, while the S was attributed to the sulfonate groups in the ZQDs. The aforementioned XPS and EDX analyses validated the existence of QDs within the hybrid CA tubular membranes.

Fig. 4 shows the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the membrane surface from the contact angle measurements. Generally, the CA pristine membrane is hydrophilic because it contains carbonyl and hydroxyl groups. The water contact angle of CQDs/CA (58.1 ± 1.4°) and TQDs/CA (55.6 ± 1.2°) were lower than the pristine membrane (60.5 ± 1.2°). The decrease in water contact angle is because incorporating quantum dots containing multiple hydrophilic functional groups (carboxyl and amino group) made the membrane more hydrophilic. The ZQDs/CA had the lowest water contact angle (54.7 ± 0.6°) because of the additional quaternary amine and sulfonate group from the ZQDs. Furthermore, the surface roughness of ZQDs/CA (Table 2) affects the hydrophilicity of the membrane because the water molecules come into contact with a larger surface area. For the underwater diesel oil contact angle, diesel oil droplets were disposed on the membrane surface. The observed trend in the diesel oil contact angle was opposite that of the water contact angle. This result implies less contact of diesel oil onto the membrane surface, resulting in a higher oil contact angle. The diesel oil contact angle of CA pristine membrane (125.1 ± 1.6°) was lower compared to CQDs/CA (126.7 ± 0.7°), TQDs/CA (130.4 ± 1.4°), and ZQDs/CA (132.3 ± 1.2°) membrane. These data indicated that introducing quantum dots into CA dope enhanced the oleophobic properties of the membrane, which gives theoretical support for diesel oil/water emulsion separation.

Fig. 5 depicts the UF performance test data on pristine and hybrid CA tubular membranes. The pristine CA tubular membrane exhibited a pure water flux (PWF) of 463.9 ± 46.2 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. The introduction of quantum dots into the membrane increased the PWF, which is attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity of the hybrid membranes (Fig. 4 (a)). Compared to the pristine CA tubular membrane, these hybrid tubular membranes exhibited larger pore size and surface porosity, rougher membrane surface, and lesser skin-layer thickness (Fig. 1 and Table 2), which allowed greater passage of water molecules and increased the PWF. Among the modified membranes, CA/ZQDs tubular membrane had the highest pure water flux (581.3 ± 33.9 L m−2 h−1 bar−1). Incorporating ZQDs introduced multiple hydrophilic functional groups (carboxyl, amine, and sulfonate) to the CA membrane, enhancing hydrophilicity and intensifying the membrane's water affinity. For the oil–water separation, integrating quantum dots into the membrane led to an increase in permeation flux. This increase was correlated to the improved oleophobic properties observed in the hybrid membranes (Fig. 4 (b)). The CA/ZQDs tubular membrane exhibited the highest permeation flux (350.5 ± 11.2 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) for reasons analogous to those influencing the PWF. All tubular membranes exhibited oil rejection rate above 97 %, indicating their exceptional efficacy in oil–water separation processes.

Fig. 6
(a) illustrates the results of antifouling tests conducted on pristine CA and ZQDs/CA tubular membranes. After three testing cycles, the ZQDs/CA tubular membrane maintained a higher water flux than the pristine CA tubular membrane. This enhanced performance can be attributed to the increased hydrophilicity of the hybrid membrane, which allows the creation of a hydration layer on their surface. This hydration layer raises the energy barrier against foulants approaching the membrane surface. It introduces steric hindrance, effectively increasing the Gibbs free energy of pollutants as they approach the membrane surface [36].

Consequently, fewer oil molecules obstructed the passage of water molecules, resulting in a higher flow of water through the membrane. These findings are consistent with those in Fig. 6 (b) and Table 3 , which show the antifouling properties (flux recovery ratio, reversible fouling ratio, and irreversible fouling ratio) of the tubular membranes. The incorporation of ZQDs led to increased flux recovery and reduced irreversible fouling; the formation of hydration layers on the hybrid membrane surface acted as a barrier against fouling. Among the membranes, ZQDs/CA exhibited the highest flux recovery (88.42 %) and reversible fouling (28.12 %) because of the enhanced hydrophilicity imparted by the ZQDs nanoparticles. Furthermore, ZQDs/CA tubular membranes displayed the lowest irreversible fouling (11.57 %) because the increased hydrophilicity reduced the attachment of oil molecules to the surface, making it easier to remove fouling contaminants during simple washing. Fig. 6 (c) and Table 3 display the FRR data for three testing cycles; after the three-cycle test, the FRR of the ZQDs/CA tubular membrane remained consistent at 77.83 %, a significant improvement over the pristine CA membrane FRR of 71.12 %. This notable enhancement can be attributed to the increased hydrophilicity and antifouling properties of the ZQDs/CA tubular membrane following the addition of ZQDs.

Table 4 shows the comparison of this work to other polymeric membranes used in oil–water separation. The results suggest that the ZQDs/CA tubular membrane was efficient for oil–water separation and might be well-suited for commercialization.

The oil–water separation performance of the ZQDs/CA tubular membrane is presented in Fig. 7 . Different types of oil-in-water emulsions such as diesel oil, dodecane, canola oil, and engine oil, were utilized to test the separation performance of the tubular membrane in oil–water separation. The ZQDs/CA tubular membrane rejection for all oil samples was greater than 94 %. This result showed that the ZQDs/CA tubular membrane exhibited excellent separation efficiency for different oil–water emulsions. The size distribution of oil droplets in the feed and permeate measured by DLS were displayed in Fig. 8 . The engine oil had the highest rejection (99.2 ± 0.4 %) because it had the highest oil droplet size. It was followed by canola oil (99.1 ± 0.2 %), then diesel oil (98.9 ± 0.4 %), and dodecane (94.2 ± 1.6 %). The trend was consistent with the size distribution of different oil–water emulsions because the large oil droplet size cannot easily penetrate the membrane's pores. In contrast, dodecane had the lowest rejection because the small oil droplet size can penetrate the membrane pores. Comparing the size distribution of the permeate and feed solution, the curve of the permeate shows one peak at about 50–100 nm, indicating that tiny oil droplets could still pass through the membrane [71,72].

Fig. 9 shows the sample images of feed (left side) and permeate (right side) for different oil–water emulsion separations using ZQDs/CA tubular membranes. It demonstrated a significant difference between the feed and permeate, where the feed was milky white in color while the permeate was transparent.

Furthermore, Fig. 10 shows the optical microscope images of the feed and permeate. The images of the feed showed the presence of oil droplets, while the images from the permeate only displayed tiny oil droplets in an almost clear solution. These results suggest that most of the oil was rejected after the feed was subjected to crossflow ultrafiltration. Overall, this shows that the CA/ZQDs tubular membrane efficiently rejects different oil–water emulsion types.

Pristine and hybrid cellulose acetate (CA) tubular membranes were fabricated using the dry-wet spinning method. ZQDs were successfully synthesized through the reaction of CQDs with EDC, followed by a ring-opening reaction via 1,4-butanesultone. The incorporation of hydrophilic quantum dots into the CA dope induced thermodynamic instability and accelerated the demixing rate, resulting in enhanced pore size, surface porosity, and hydrophilicity of the membranes. The ZQDs/CA tubular membrane demonstrated superior ultrafiltration performance, with notable values for pure water flux (581.3 ± 33.9 L m−2 h−1 bar−1), permeation flux (581.3 ± 33.9 L m−2h−1 bar−1), and separation efficiency (>98 %). Moreover, it exhibited commendable antifouling characteristics (flux recovery ratio = 88.42 %; reversible fouling ratio = 28.12 %; irreversible fouling ratio = 11.57 %). Additionally, the ZQDs/CA tubular membrane achieved a high rejection rate (94–99 %) for various types of oil–water emulsions (diesel, dodecane, canola, and engine oil). In summary, the ZQDs/CA tubular membranes proved effective in the separation of oil and water.
